text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Ayesha Rahman
Southside Health Centre

12 Brook Lane, London SE15 3QT
Dear Dr Ayesha Rahman
Amelia Carter, DOB: 14-03-1996, Hospital No: H928417 NHS No. 8035619240
Flat 3, 29 Alder Grove, London SE23 2LP 

Diagnoses/summary: Generalised epilepsy with catamenial pattern suspected; patient-reported clustering around menses. Long-standing history of absence and generalised tonic–clonic seizures (GTCs). Previous EEGs demonstrating generalised spike–wave discharges; no focal onset features elicited. 

Neuroimaging: MRI brain (2021) structurally normal, no focal cortical dysplasia or mesial temporal sclerosis identified.

Seizure burden: As per diary and menstrual tracking app entries, sz Xfour/wk on average over the last 8 weeks, with clear peri-menstrual exacerbation (days −3 to +3). Occasional early-morning myoclonic jerks reported. No recent injuries; one tongue bite last month; no prolonged post-ictal confusion beyond 20–30 minutes. 

Safety/social: Not driving. No current pregnancies. Uses period-tracking app (patient consented to share summary logs) showing cycles 28–31 days; seizure clusters align with luteal–perimenstrual window. Sleep disruption and stress noted as additional triggers.

Current medication: 
- Sodium valproate MR 500 mg twice daily (weight neutral to +1 kg; notes mild hair change and tremor; contraception discussed previously)
- Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily (mood irritability manageable)
- Folic Acid 5 mg daily
- PRN buccal midazolam supplied but not used in past 6 months

Previous medication: Lamotrigine (breakthrough GTCs and rash), Topiramate (word-finding difficulty, paresthesias), Zonisamide (insomnia, anorexia), Clobazam (sedation), Ethosuximide (partial benefit for absences only), Perampanel (mood lability). 

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Background today: I reviewed Amelia Carter in clinic, who attended alone. She describes increasing seizure clustering around menstruation over the past 3 cycles, corroborated by her app screenshots. Fatigue and low mood premenstrually. She is otherwise well; no intercurrent infections. Sleep averaged 6 hours/night due to shift work. She denies alcohol or recreational drugs. She queries non-hormonal strategies and whether cycle-linked dose adjustments could help. She is aware of pregnancy considerations with valproate and confirms effective contraception in place. 

Examination: General and neurological examination normal between seizures. Weight 67 kg, BP 112/68. No cognitive slowing on casual testing; speech fluent. 

Discussion: We discussed that she has generalised epilepsy with probable catamenial modulation. The options covered included lifestyle optimisation (sleep regularity, stress reduction), short-cycle benzodiazepine bridging, or targeted dose adjustments around the high-risk window. We also touched on hormonal strategies via Gynaecology, noting risks/benefits. She prefers to try non-hormonal measures first, with careful monitoring. Side-effects, SUDEP information, safety around water/heights/cooking, and rescue plan were reiterated. 

Plan:
- Continue sodium valproate MR 500 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily for now. 
- Trial short intermittent clobazam 10 mg nocte from day −3 to +3 of menses for two cycles, if tolerated; counselled on sedation and driving restrictions (not currently driving). 
- Sleep hygiene measures; advise consistent bed/wake times even on shift days, consider rota discussion with employer if feasible.
- Continue folic acid 5 mg daily. Contraception to remain effective; avoid pregnancy on current regimen; preconception counselling offered when appropriate. 
- Bloods: FBC, U&E, LFTs, valproate trough level in 2 weeks; thyroid function given fatigue.
- Provide fit note if clusters interfere with work; patient to keep detailed seizure–cycle diary in the app and share PDFs prior to next review.
- Rescue plan: midazolam buccal as previously instructed for any convulsive seizure >5 minutes or seizure clusters as per plan; partner aware. 
- Refer to Gynaecology for discussion of hormonal options if inadequate response to intermittent strategy. 
- Follow-up: nurse specialist telephone review in 6 weeks to assess tolerability and impact on seizure clustering; face-to-face review in 4 months, sooner if deterioration. 

Additional notes: We confirmed safeguarding and home safety measures. She understands to seek urgent care for prolonged seizures or significant increase in frequency beyond her baseline sz Xfour/wk. She consented to the plan and data sharing of app summaries within her record. Some entries showed "Ã‚Â·" and "â€“" garbled symbols in exported text, but dates and counts were clear. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: sz Xfour/wk on average over the last 8 weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter includes an explicit numeric seizure frequency: "sz Xfour/wk on average over the last 8 weeks" under Seizure burden. Interpreting 'Xfour/wk' as '4 per week'. No other numeric frequency contradicts or exceeds this. Clusters are mentioned but not quantified beyond the average weekly rate. Therefore, the highest/clearest frequency is 4 per week.
seizure_frequency_number: sz Xfour/wk on average over the last 8 weeks
